lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.description: Start every conversation right. These 8 phrases cover everything from hello to sorry — and will make locals smile the moment you use them.
lessons.culturalNote: "Have you eaten yet?" (Lí chia̍h-pá--bôe?) is one of the most common Hokkien greetings — even more so than 'Hello'. It reflects how central food and communal eating are in Hokkien culture. Answering "chia̍h-pá ah!" (eaten already!) is perfectly normal even as a casual acknowledgement.

etymologyNotes.note: Literally "bad situation" — 歹 (pháiⁿ) = bad/ugly, 勢 (sè) = situation/position. It conveys social awkwardness rather than deep guilt, making it ideal for minor apologies and bumping into people.
Shares characters with Mandarin 再見 (zàijiàn) — 再 = again, 見 = to see/meet. A cross-dialect phrase that highlights the shared written heritage of Chinese languages.

lessons.title: Introductions
lessons.description: Tell people who you are, where you're from, and how to respond when they ask. The building blocks of every new friendship.
lessons.culturalNote: Hokkien speakers often greet strangers by asking where you're from (Lí tó-uī lâng?) — this is friendly curiosity, not intrusion. Knowing whether someone is Taiwanese, Penang Hokkien, or Singaporean tells you a lot about which words and tones they use.

etymologyNotes.note: Literally "no need for guest air" — 客氣 (kheh-khì) means formality or ceremony (lit. guest-spirit). Telling someone m̄-bián kheh-khì means "relax, we're not strangers".
Hòaⁿ-hí (歡喜) = happy/joyful. Jīn-bat (認識) = to recognise + to know. Literally "joyful to recognise you" — a more layered phrase than it first appears.

lessons.title: Communication
lessons.description: The most important toolkit for any learner — how to say you're confused, ask someone to slow down, and tell them you're still learning.
lessons.culturalNote: Telling a native speaker "góa iáu teh o̍h Hô-ló-uē" (I'm still learning Hokkien) almost always gets a warm, encouraging reaction. Hokkien speakers deeply appreciate the effort — many never expect outsiders to know a single word.

etymologyNotes.note: Thiaⁿ-bô literally means "hear + nothing" — the sound reaches you but nothing registers. Compare thiaⁿ-tio̍h (heard it successfully) or thiaⁿ-kàu (heard to completion).
Chai-iáⁿ (知影) = know + shadow/image. To understand something is to have a mental image of it — a beautifully visual metaphor for comprehension unique to Min languages.

lessons.title: Getting Around
lessons.description: Navigate confidently — left, right, straight ahead, and how to ask for directions without pulling out your phone.
lessons.culturalNote: In older Hokkien-speaking communities, directions are sometimes given relative to natural features — "towards the mountain" or "towards the sea" — rather than left/right. In night markets and hawker centres, a pointed finger and "chêng-bīn" (ahead) does the job.

etymologyNotes.note: 倒 (tò) means reversed or upside-down — the "opposite" side. In many Chinese dialects, left is the "reverse" direction, reflecting an ancient cultural preference for the right side.
正 (chiàⁿ) means correct, upright, or proper — the right side is literally the "correct" side. This mirrors many world cultures where right = correct/righteous.

lessons.title: Food & Shopping
lessons.description: Order food, ask prices, haggle at the market, and tell your host you're stuffed. Hokkien food culture is legendary — these phrases unlock it.
lessons.culturalNote: Saying siuⁿ-kùi (too expensive) in a market is expected and not rude — it opens a negotiation. Sellers often wait for it. Pairing it with "Góa beh bóe nn̄g-ê" (I'll buy two) dramatically improves your chances.

etymologyNotes.note: Siuⁿ (傷) literally means "excessively" or "to excess" — the same character used for injury. So siuⁿ-kùi = painfully expensive. A dramatic but extremely common intensifier in everyday Hokkien.
Pak-tó͘ (腹肚) = belly-stomach. Iau (枵) = hungry/hollow. Literally "my belly is hollow" — a vivid, physical expression unique to Hokkien and related Min languages.

lessons.title: Review: Lessons 1–5
lessons.description: Mix quiz across all five lessons you've completed. Spaced retrieval across a varied pool is proven to double long-term retention — this is your first big checkpoint.
lessons.culturalNote: In Hokkien culture, repetition is respected — not seen as a sign of weakness. The saying 熟讀唐詩三百首 (practice until it flows like water) captures the attitude: fluency comes from cycling back, not just moving forward.

lessons.title: Numbers
lessons.description: Count from zero to a thousand. Numbers unlock prices, times, ages, and quantities — the backbone of any practical conversation.
lessons.culturalNote: Hokkien numbers are compound: 11 = cha̍p-it (ten-one), 21 = jī-cha̍p-it (two-ten-one). But watch out — 4 (sì) sounds like 死 (sí, death), so many buildings skip level 4. And the word for 8 (peh) sounds like 發 (hoat, prosperity), making it very auspicious for prices and phone numbers.

etymologyNotes.note: 千 (chheng) shares its root with Mandarin 千 (qiān). Notice the shift: Hokkien preserved the older ts-/ch- initial that Mandarin softened to q-. This phonological conservatism is a hallmark of Min languages.
Hokkien 'one' splits into two forms: chit (as a counter — chit-ê = one item) and it (in compounds like it-pah = one hundred). This tonal variation based on grammatical role is unique to Southern Min.

lessons.title: Family
lessons.description: Family is everything in Hokkien culture. Master these terms and locals will light up when you use them — especially the rich system of grandparent and sibling words.
lessons.culturalNote: Hokkien has one of the most elaborate family term systems in the world — separate words for every relative by side of family, birth order, and generation. Just using a-kong (grandpa) and a-má (grandma) correctly will earn instant warmth from elders in any Hokkien community.

etymologyNotes.note: 阿爸 uses 阿 (a-) as an affectionate prefix — the same particle you find in a-má, a-kong, a-chek, a-kó. It has no direct translation but marks kinship terms as warm and familiar rather than formal.

朋友 (pêng-iú) is shared across Sinitic languages. But note: in Hokkien social settings, calling someone 朋友 is quite formal. The casual word for close friend or mate varies by region — in Penang you often hear 伙伴 (hóe-phōaⁿ).

lessons.title: How Are You Feeling?
lessons.description: Go beyond 'I'm fine'. Express happiness, tiredness, cold, heat, and illness — the real emotional vocabulary that makes conversations feel human.
lessons.culturalNote: Hokkien emotional expression is often indirect — instead of saying 'I'm angry', people often describe the physical feeling (my heart is on fire, my stomach is upset). The word 歡喜 (hoaⁿ-hí, happy) is also used to mean 'pleased' and 'willing' — so 'I'm happy to do it' and 'I like it' use the same word.

etymologyNotes.note: 歡喜 = 歡 (hoaⁿ, joyful) + 喜 (hí, delighted). Two joy-characters stacked together. This word appears in the Buddhist phrase 歡喜心 (hoaⁿ-hí-sim) — 'joyful heart' — central to Hokkien religious culture.

生氣 (seⁿ-khì) literally means 'grow air/energy'. In traditional Chinese medicine, anger causes qi (energy) to rise upward through the body — so being angry is described as producing excess qi. The same metaphor is used in Mandarin (shēngqì).

lessons.title: Time & Days
lessons.description: Talk about when — today, tomorrow, what time, morning and night. Essential for making plans, catching transport, and booking anything.
lessons.culturalNote: Hokkien time expressions often stack naturally: kin-á-jit = today (this + day), bîn-á-chài = tomorrow (bright + morning + carry). Noticing these compound structures makes the whole system intuitive — you're not memorising separate words, you're learning a pattern.

etymologyNotes.note: 明仔載 = 明 (bîn, bright/dawn) + 仔 (á, diminutive particle) + 載 (chài, carry/occasion). Literally 'the bright little occasion that's coming' — one of the most vivid time words in any dialect.

幾點 = kúi (how many) + tiám (dots/points). Time in Hokkien is counted in 'dots' on a clock face — the same metaphor as in Mandarin (幾點 jǐ diǎn). The particle 'ah' softens it from a demand into a casual question.

lessons.title: The Body
lessons.description: Know your body parts in Hokkien — vital for describing pain, visiting a clinic, or just understanding the colourful idioms that reference body parts constantly.
lessons.culturalNote: Body-part idioms are everywhere in Hokkien. A thick-faced person (bīn phòng-phòng) is shameless. Someone with a hot hand (chhiú joa̍h) is on a lucky streak. Knowing the words unlocks a whole register of vivid everyday expressions.

etymologyNotes.note: 痛 (thiàⁿ) has two readings in Hokkien: thiàⁿ for physical pain (it hurts), and same character for emotional pain — 痛疼 (thiàⁿ-thàng, to love dearly/feel for someone). So the word for pain and the word for deep parental love share the same root.

跤 (kha) is a uniquely Southern Min character. Kha appears in dozens of everyday compounds: kha-chhiú (hands and feet = followers), chia̍h-kha (eating leg = the one who eats into the budget), thô͘-kha (ground-foot = floor/ground).

lessons.title: Review: Lessons 6–10
lessons.description: The second major checkpoint — a mixed quiz across numbers, family, feelings, time, and body. Completing this unlocks the Survival Pack bonus lesson.
lessons.culturalNote: Reaching this point means you have the vocabulary for a real conversation. The Penang Hokkien community has a saying: 講話免驚錯 — Don't be afraid of making mistakes when you speak. Every error is remembered more than ten correct sentences.

lessons.title: Survival Pack
lessons.description: Eight phrases that could genuinely help you in an emergency or awkward situation. Learn these last — and hope you never need them.
lessons.culturalNote: In many Hokkien-speaking regions, shouting 救人啊! (Kiù-lâng--ah! — Save people!) is universally understood as a distress call. Locals will respond faster to this than to 'help' in English, especially in night markets, temples, and older neighbourhoods.

etymologyNotes.note: 救 (kiù) = to rescue/save. 人 (lâng) = person(s). --ah is a sentence-final particle that makes it urgent and exclamatory. Literally 'Save people!' — the same phrasing used in classical texts and modern emergencies alike.

便所 = 便 (piān, convenience/ease) + 所 (só͘, place). Literally 'convenience place' — a formal and polite term. In casual Hokkien you might also hear 屎礐仔 (sái-hak-á, the excrement pit) — considerably less polite but widely understood.

lessons.title: Weather & Nature
lessons.description: Talk about the sky, the seasons, and the natural world around you. Essential vocabulary for daily small-talk in any Hokkien community.
lessons.culturalNote: Weather is a cornerstone of Hokkien small-talk, especially in Malaysia and Singapore where rain can arrive suddenly and intensely. Commenting on the weather — 落雨了 (Lo̍h-hō͘--ah, it's raining!) — is a perfectly natural conversation opener with any elder or neighbour.

etymologyNotes.note: 雨 (hō͘) — the Hokkien reading of 雨 is hō͘ (tone 7), which differs from Mandarin yǔ. The same character in compounds: 落雨 (lo̍h-hō͘, to rain) and 雨傘 (hō͘-soaⁿ, umbrella).
月 (goe̍h/ge̍h) — also means 'month', just like in Mandarin. 月光 (goe̍h-kng) = moonlight. The same character gives us 月餅 (goe̍h-piáⁿ, mooncake) central to the Mid-Autumn Festival.

lessons.title: At the Market
lessons.description: Bargain, ask prices, and shop confidently at any wet market, hawker centre, or pasar malam. These phrases will save you money and earn you respect.
lessons.culturalNote: Bargaining (講價 kóng-kè) is expected and respected at wet markets throughout Southeast Asia. Opening with 'Jōa-chōe chîⁿ?' (How much?) followed by a polite counter-offer is a social skill as much as a financial one. Elderly vendors often light up when you speak Hokkien.

etymologyNotes.note: 貴 (kùi) — in Hokkien, 貴 covers both 'expensive' and 'honourable'. The same word in the polite question 'What is your honourable surname?' (Lí kùi-sìⁿ?) shows how price and status share the same ancient character.

市 (chhī) — 菜市 (chhài-chhī, vegetable market), 夜市 (iā-chhī, night market). The wet market is the social centre of any traditional Hokkien neighbourhood — a place for news, gossip, and community connection as much as shopping.

lessons.title: Home & Daily Routines
lessons.description: The language of everyday home life — from waking up to locking the door. Perfect for talking with family or describing your daily routine.
lessons.culturalNote: The traditional Hokkien home (厝 chhù) is more than a building — it reflects ancestral ties and family hierarchy. Many Hokkien families still use the same house terminology that would be understood by ancestors from Fujian hundreds of years ago.

etymologyNotes.note: 厝 (chhù) — a distinctly Southern Min character rarely used in standard Chinese. The ancestral home (祖厝 tsó͘-chhù) is sacred in Hokkien culture; many families return to the family 厝 for major festivals even generations after leaving.

起身 (khí-sin) — literally 'rise body'. A gentle phrase used within the home. Compare with the more abrupt Mandarin 起床 (qǐchuáng). The softness of khí-sin reflects the relational warmth of Hokkien domestic language.

lessons.title: Health & Emergencies
lessons.description: The most important words you hope you never need — but must know. From minor ailments to emergencies, these phrases could make all the difference.
lessons.culturalNote: Traditional Hokkien communities often combine modern medicine with traditional remedies (草藥 chháu-io̍h). Before visiting a clinic, many elders will recommend herbal soups and rest. Knowing how to describe your symptoms in Hokkien helps bridge the gap with older family members and traditional practitioners.

etymologyNotes.note: 藥 (io̍h) — used in both Western medicine (藥水 io̍h-chúi, liquid medicine) and traditional remedies (草藥 chháu-io̍h, herbal medicine). The character 藥 contains 草 (grass/herbs) at the top, a reminder of its botanical origins.
疼/痛 (thiàⁿ) — also means 'to love' or 'to cherish' in Hokkien. A parent saying 'Góa thiàⁿ lí' (我疼你) means 'I love you'. Pain and love share the same word — a poignant feature of the language.

lessons.title: Describing Things
lessons.description: Hokkien adjectives go straight after the noun they describe — no need for a verb 'to be'. Ten essential adjectives that unlock expressive, natural-sounding Hokkien.
lessons.culturalNote: In Hokkien, adjectives function as stative verbs — 'chit-ê lâng súi' (這個人媠, this person beautiful) is grammatically complete without a verb 'to be'. This is a key structural difference from English that makes Hokkien adjectives feel more vivid and direct.

etymologyNotes.note: 媠 (súi) — a character specific to Taiwanese Hokkien script, rarely found in standard Chinese dictionaries. In Malaysia it is also commonly written as 媠 or the borrowed character 美 (bí). It describes beauty in people, objects and scenery alike.

辣 (hiam/la̍t) or 熱 (jia̍t) — Hokkien distinguishes clearly between spicy-hot (辣 hiam) and temperature-hot (熱 jia̍t). Knowing both is essential at any hawker stall: 'bē-hiam' (not spicy) vs 'ài-hiam' (want spicy) are phrases you will use every meal.

lessons.title: Review: Lessons 13–17
lessons.description: Consolidate everything from the last five lessons: nature, the market, home life, health, and descriptions.
